# 1. Introduction


Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which is the final or one of the byproducts of many environmental and biological reactions, plays a crucial role in the areas of pharmaceutical, biological, and environmental analysis. 1,2The concentration of the target product can be determined as soon as the concentration of H2O2is detected. For example, H2O2is the final product of the acetylcholine decomposition reaction under catalysis by acetylcholineesterase and cholinesterase, and the content of acetylcholine can be calculated according to the detected concentration of H2O2. 3There are many analytical methods, such as chemiluminescence, 4electrochemistry, 5fluorescence, 6bioelectrochemistry, 7liquid chromatography, 8and colorimetric methods, 9that have been developed to monitor hydrogen peroxide; among these determined methodologies, the colorimetric method has attracted interest because it is a time saving, low-cost, simple, and convenient method for practical applications. Furthermore, the detection of H2O2using the colorimetric method can be immediately interpreted using the naked eye to realize visual

# Synthesis of well-dispersed Fe3O4nanoparticles loaded on montmorillonite and sensitive colorimetric detection of H2O2based on its peroxidase-like activity


Kaili Wu, a Xin Zhao, a Miaomiao Chen, a Huawei Zhang, a Zhenxue Liu, a Xiao Zhang, b Xixi Zhu* a and Qingyun Liu* a

In this study, a simple strategy for the preparation of Fe3O4nanoparticles (NPs) loaded on montmorillonite as a support is reported. The Fe3O4-MMT nanocomposites (NCs) were demonstrated to possess peroxidase-like activity and could catalyze the oxidation of3,3 0 ,5,5 0 -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to a blue oxidation product (oxTMB) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide. The obvious color change can be used to quantify the H2O2concentration using UV-vis spectroscopy. In addition, the Fe3O4-MMT NCs exhibit enhanced peroxidase-like activity as compared to MMT or pure Fe3O4NPs. Steady-state kinetics studies and a hydroxyl radical detecting assay were carried out to investigate the peroxidase-like activity and catalytic mechanism of these NCs. Under the optimized conditions, Fe3O4-MMT NCs are expected to be used as a good colorimetric biosensor for the detection of H2O2, which have a linear range from5 /C2 10 /C0 5M to2 /C2 10 /C0 4M with a detectable H2O2concentration as low as5.1 /C2 10 /C0 6M. This sensor was also successfully applied to detect H2O2in contact lens solution samples.

detection without any expensive or sophisticated instrumentation. 10Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), which can be used to detect H2O2, has great practical applications due to its good selectivity and high sensitivity; however, as a natural enzyme, it has some intrinsic drawbacks, including the sensitivity of its catalytic activity to the environmental conditions, ease of denaturation and deactivation, high cost, difficulties in preparation and purification, and time-consuming reaction process, 11-13that have hindered its practical application.

With the development of nanoscience and nanotechnology, numerous artificial enzymes, which possess unique optical, electronic, magnetic, and catalytic properties, have attracted significant attention in recent years as they can overcome the limitations of natural enzymes to some extent. 14For example, Fe3O4nanoparticles, 15as the first reported artificial enzyme, were found to possess an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. After this, the application of nanomaterials as artificial enzymes has been explored significantly. Due to the intriguing features and applications of Fe3O4nanoparticles, many preparation methods, including co-precipitation, 16thermal decomposition, 17polyol route, 18hydrothermal process, 19,20and solvothermal technique, 21,22have been reported for their synthesis. Furthermore, various nanostructures, such as zero-dimensional nanoparticles (nanospheres, nanooctahedrons, and nanocubes), 23-25one-dimensional nanostructures (nanorods, nanowires, and nanotubes), and two-dimensional nanostructures (nanorings, nanoplates, and nanoprisms), of Fe3O4

nanoparticles have been prepared. 26-31Nanomaterials have gradually played a significant role in the areas of catalysis based on their small size effect, high specific surface area, quantum size effect, etc. Moreover, metal oxide nanomaterials, such as Co3O4nanoparticles, 32-35CuOnanoclusters, 36,37and V2O5nanowires, 38have attracted significant interest due to controllability in their synthesis process.

However, Fe3O4NPs easily aggregate due to their magnetic attractive forces and long-range van der Waals attractive forces. Therefore, it is necessary to employ a support to solve this problem. Montmorillonite (MMT), as an important class of natural host-guest layered materials, can be used as a support to decrease the high specific surface energy of nanoparticles. Due to its inherent advantages including high chemical and mechanical stability, low-cost, a well-defined layered structure, large surface area, superior adsorption ability, high cation exchange capacity, and non-toxicity, MMT has been widely applied in catalysts, electrodes, sensors, and antibacterial agents. In addition, MMT is one of the important functionalized layered silicates, which can be easily intercalated or exfoliated. Its biological compatibility is the foundation for the preparation of numerous composites and their application in biological systems. To date, many kinds of nanoparticles, such as metal (Cu, Ag, Pd, Pt, and Co), 39-42metal oxide (TiO2, ZnO, Co3O4, and CeO2), 43-46and metal sulfide (Ag2S, CuS, and ZnS) nanoparticles, 47-49have been dispersed on the surface of MMT to prevent their aggregation. However, to the best of our knowledge, studies on the use of MMT as a support for Fe3O4NPs that demonstrate enhanced peroxidase-like activity and applications in the detection of H2O2have not been reported to date.

Herein, Fe3O4NPs were anchored on the surface of MMT using a facile hydrothermal method and exhibited peroxidase mimetic activity for the first time. The Fe3O4-MMT nanocomposites can catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2, and the system changed from colorless to blue only in5min. The catalytic mechanism involved hydroxyl radicals, originating due to the rapid decomposition of hydrogen peroxide after the addition of Fe3O4-MMT nanocomposites. Herein, a colorimetric sensor for the detection of H2O2was developed based on the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4-MMT nanocomposites (Scheme1).


# 2. Experimental



# 2.1Materials


Montmorillonite (MMT) and3,3 0 ,5,5 0 -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Solarbio (Tianjin, China). Sodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7 /C1 2H2O), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), and ferrous sulfate (FeSO4 /C1 7H2O) were purchased from Guangcheng Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrogen peroxide (30wt%, H2O2), sodium acetate (NaAc), and acetic acid (HAc) were purchased from Gucheng Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Terephthalic acid (TA) was purchased from Chengdu cologne chemical reagent Co. All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

Scheme1A schematic of the colorimetric detection of H2O2catalyzed by the Fe3O4-MMT NCs.


# 2.2Characterization


The morphologies of the nanocomposites were investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) via a field-emission microscope (JSM7600F). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of200kV to study the morphology and size of the product. The phase of the obtained Fe3O4-MMT nanocomposites was characterized via powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) conducted using a D/max2550VB/PC diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) with Cu K a radiation ( l = 0.15418nm); Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed using a NICOLET380Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Nicolet Thermo, USA) with compressed pellets obtained by mixing the sample with KBr. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a scanning X-ray microprobe (ESCSLAB250Xi, ThermoFisher, USA), and the binding energy (BE) was calibrated using the C (1s) peak at284.6eV. UV-vis absorption spectra were obtained using the MAPADA UV-8000PC spectrophotometer (Beijing, China). The fluorometric data were acquired using the Cary Eclipse spectrofluorophotometer (Varian, Inc., USA).


# 2.3Synthesis of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs


The Fe3O4-MMT NCs were prepared according to a literature procedure with some modification. 50The modified procedure was as follows: MMT (0.25g) was dispersed in distilled water (10mL) under vigorous stirring for18h to form a welldispersed MMT dispersion. Subsequently, an aqueous solution of FeSO4 /C1 7H2O (1mL, 3M) was added dropwise to the MMT dispersion and violently stirred for another1h to ensure that Fe2+ was well dispersed on the surface of MMT. An aqueous solution (19mL) containing sodium citrate (1.5mM), NaOH (6mM), and NaNO3 (0.3M) was heated to100 1C to form a pellucid solution and rapidly added to the MMT dispersion (equivalent to0.10M Fe2+ in the alkaline pellucid solution). Finally, the mixed solution was transferred quickly to a Teflonlined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at100 1C for1h. The solution was then naturally cooled down to room

temperature; the products were separated from the solution using a magnet and washed several times with deionized water. Finally, the products were obtained and dried under atmospheric conditions at50 1C for10h. The obtained Fe3O4-MMT NCs were used in the following experiments.

For comparative investigation, pure Fe3O4NPs were also prepared using a similar procedure as that used to prepare the Fe3O4-MMT NCs in the absence of MMT.


# 2.4Catalytic oxidation of TMB using the Fe3O4-MMT NCs


The peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs was investigated by measuring the catalytic oxidation of the peroxidase substrate TMB in the presence of H2O2. A typical colorimetric analysis was realized as follows: 200m L of TMB (1mM) in a sodium acetate buffer solution (pH3.8) was subsequently treated with (a) 200m L of30% H2O2 (0.25M) and Fe3O4-MMT NCs (200m L, 0.3mg mL /C0 1 ), (b) 200m L of30% H2O2 (0.25M) and Fe3O4NPs (200m L, 0.3mg mL /C0 1 ), (c) 200m L of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs (0.3mg mL /C0 1 ), (d) 200m L of30% H2O2 (0.25M) and MMT (200m L, 0.3mg mL /C0 1 ), (e) 200m L of30% H2O2 (0.25M), and (f) no addition of H2O2and Fe3O4-MMT NCs. Subsequently, in each of the systems, the total volume was2mL, which was incubated for5min at room temperature and then measured by monitoring the absorbance in the wavelength-scan mode from500to800nm.


# 2.5Kinetics measurements


The reaction kinetics studies were implemented using a UV-vis absorbance spectrophotometer in the time-scan mode. The complete catalytic experiments were carried out as follows: 200m L of the Fe3O4-MMT NC solution (0.3mg mL /C0 1 ) in the HAc-NaAc buffer solution (1.4mL, pH4) was treated with0.2mL of TMB at different concentrations (0.01-0.1mM) and0.2mL of H2O2 (0.25M) or0.2mL of H2O2at different concentrations (1-10mM) and TMB (1mM) with a total reaction volume of2mL at room temperature. The Michaelis-Menten constant ( Km) was calculated according to the Lineweaver-Burk plot: 1/ n = Km/ Vmax(1/[S] + 1/ Km), where n is the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, Km is the Michaelis constant, and [S] is the concentration of the substrate. 51


# 2.6Rapid colorimetric detection of H2O2using the Fe3O4-MMT NCs


Fe3O4-MMT, as a peroxidase mimic, can be used for the colorimetric detection of H2O2. The typical experiments were performed by measuring the catalytic oxidation of the peroxidase substrate TMB(0.2mL, 1mM) in the presence of H2O2 (0.2mL, 1-1000m M) together with1.4mL of sodium acetate buffer solution (pH4) and0.2mL of Fe3O4-MMT NC solution (0.3mg mL /C0 1 ).


# 3. Results and discussion



# 3.1Characterization of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs


Fig. 1shows the XRD patterns of MMT (Fig. 1a), Fe3O4NPs (Fig. 1b), and Fe3O4-MMT NCs (Fig. 1c). Fig. 1b shows the XRD

Fig. 1The XRD spectra of MMT (a), Fe3O4NPs (b), and Fe3O4-MMT NCs (c).

pattern of the as-prepared Fe3O4NPs without MMT as a support. In addition, the Fe3O4NPs exhibited a highly crystalline cubic spinel structure, and the diffraction peaks (2y ) at30.1 1 , 35.5 1 , 43.2 1 , 53.5 1 , 57.2 1 , and62.8 1corresponded well to the [220], [311], [400], [422], [511], and [440] planes of the cubic Fe3O4lattice (JCPDS75-1610). 52From Fig. 1c, it can be found that the characteristic peaks of the Fe3O4NPs in the Fe3O4-MMT NCs are still in accordance with those of the pure Fe3O4NPs (Fig. 1b); this suggests that the Fe3O4NPs are deposited on the surface of MMT, and the Fe3O4-MMT NCs have been successfully obtained.

Fig. 2shows the SEM and TEM images of the Fe3O4NPs (Fig. 2A and C) and Fe3O4-MMT NCs (Fig. 2B and D), respectively. In Fig. 2A, it can be seen that the Fe3O4NPs aggregate heavily with an inhomogeneous morphology due to their magnetic properties and the long-range van der Waals attractive forces. Notably,

Fig. 2The SEM and TEM images of the Fe3O4NPs (A and C) and Fe3O4-MMT NCs (B and D), respectively.

Fig. 3An image of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs dispersed in water (A) and their response to an external magnetic field (B).

after introducing MMT as a support, the Fe3O4NPs were found to be well-dispersed on the surface of MMT (Fig. 2B and D); this indicated that MMT could promote the dispersion of the Fe3O4NPs.

The dispersion results of the as-prepared Fe3O4-MMT NCs in water and when subjected to an external magnetic field are exhibited in Fig. 3A and B, respectively. It can be seen that the as-obtained Fe3O4-MMT NCs not only have a good dispersity in water (Fig. 3A) but can also be attracted by a magnetic bar and demonstrate better magnetic properties (Fig. 3B). Thus, the Fe3O4-MMT NCs can be easily separated from the solution using an external magnetic field. Moreover, the solution became transparent after being attracted by a magnetic bar for1h; this indicated that the Fe3O4NPs had been securely anchored onto MMT.

The FTIR spectra of MMT, Fe3O4, and the Fe3O4-MMT NCs are shown in Fig. 4. In Fig. 4b, it can be seen that the vibration at578cm /C0 1can be assigned to the vibration of Fe-O, which is also found in the Fe3O4-MMT nanocomposites (Fig. 4c). 53,54In Fig. 4a and c, it can be seen that the vibration bands at

Fig. 4The FTIR spectra of MMT (a), Fe3O4 (b), and Fe3O4-MMT nanocomposites (c).

3624and795cm /C0 1exist in MMT and the Fe3O4-MMT nanocomposites corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the Al-OH and Si-O groups, respectively. 55The bands at914, 844, 520, and463cm /C0 1observed for MMT and the Fe3O4-MMT nanocomposites were attributed to the deformation vibrations of the Al-Al-OH, Al-Mg-OH, Si-O-Al, and Si-O-Fe, respectively. 56The bands at ca. 3433and1643cm /C0 1are attributed to the OH stretching and deformation of water, respectively. 57The vibration bands at ca. 1382and1558cm /C0 1of the Fe3O4nanoparticles were attributed to the symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching vibrations of the carboxyl groups of sodium citrate, respectively. Compared with that of MMT, most of the vibrations of the functional groups of MMT in the Fe3O4-MMT nanocomposites are present with only a minor change; this suggests that the interaction occurs between MMT and Fe3O4.

To discuss the oxidation states of the metal oxide in the composites, the Fe3O4-MMT nanocomposites were analyzed using XPS, as shown in Fig. 5. The wide-scan spectrum (Fig. 5A) confirmed the signal binding energy of Fe2p, Si2p, and Al2p. As shown in Fig. 5B, the binding energies of Fe2p at710.8eV and724.2eV are characteristic of Fe2p3/2and Fe2p1/2, respectively, demonstrating that iron oxides in the nanocomposites are Fe3O4, which is consistent with the XRD data. 58The peaks centered at74.2eV and102.36eV correspond to Al2p (Fig. 5C) and Si2p (Fig. 5D), respectively.


# 3.2Peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs


The peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs was evaluated using the catalytic oxidation of TMB, a representative peroxidase substrate, in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 6a, when the Fe3O4-MMT NCs were added to the H2O2 + TMB system, a distinct colour change (from colourless to blue) in the TMB + Fe3O4-MMT NCs + H2O2system was observed by the naked eye within5min (Scheme1). However, the experimental system containing the Fe3O4NPs merely exhibited a weaker color change as compared to the TMB + Fe3O4-MMT NCs + H2O2system, as shown in Fig. 6b. In addition, the control experiments (Fig. 6a, b and d) showed that the catalytic activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs was higher than that of the Fe3O4NPs or MMT alone. In Fig. 6c, it can be seen that the TMB + Fe3O4MMTNCsystem does not exhibit any color change; this suggests that the Fe3O4-MMT NCs do not oxidize the TMB substrate without H2O2. The results verify that the Fe3O4-MMT NCs possess an enhanced peroxidase-like catalytic activity and can effectively catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2. Considering that the catalytic reaction can be detected by monitoring the change in the TMB absorbance at632nm, the corresponding UV-vis spectra further reveal different color changes in different reaction systems. Similar to the color change, a very strong absorbance at632nm can be observed in the TMB + H2O2 + Fe3O4-MMT NC system.


# 3.3Optimization of the experimental conditions


Similar to that of the natural enzyme HRP59,60and other nanomaterial-based peroxidase mimics, 15the catalytic activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs depends on the temperature and pH.

Fig. 5The XPS spectra obtained for the Fe3O4-MMT NCs: wide-scan (A), Fe2p (B), Al2p (C), and Si2p (D) spectra.

Fig. 6The UV-vis absorption spectra (from500to800nm) obtained for the different reaction systems: Fe3O4-MMT NCs + H2O2 + TMB (a), pure Fe3O4nanoparticles + H2O2 + TMB (b), Fe3O4-MMT NCs + TMB (c), MMT + H2O2 + TMB (d), H2O2 + TMB (e), and TMB (f). The inset corresponds to the color variation of the different experimental systems.

The enhanced peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs was investigated by varying the temperature from15 1C to80 1C and pH from2.0to8.0. In Fig. 7A, it can be seen that the Fe3O4MMTNCs retain a quite high catalytic activity from50to55 1C;

|

this suggests that the Fe3O4-MMT NCs possess a high temperature resistance. As shown in Fig. 7B, the catalytic activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs increased upon increasing the pH from2.0to4.0and then decreased with a further increase in pH. Thus, the optimal pH was approximately4.0, indicating that the oxidation of TMB occurred easily under acidic conditions. 61,62


# 3.4Kinetic studies of the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs


To further investigate the peroxidase-like behavior of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs, the apparent steady-state kinetic parameters ( Km and Vmax) for the initial reaction that conformed to the typical Michaelis-Menten model were determined. The experiments were carried out in a suitable concentration range of TMB or H2O2as substrates by changing the concentration of one substrate and keeping that of the other constant (Fig. 8A and B). All the parameters were also regressed to the Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal model (1/ v0vs. 1/[S]), which also provided analogous values (Fig. 8C and D). As shown in Table1, the Km value of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs with H2O2as the substrate was0.811mM, much lower than that of HRP (3.70mM) reported in the literature, indicating that the Fe3O4MMTNCshad a slightly better affinity towards H2O2than HRP. Moreover, the Km value with TMB (1mM) as the substrate

Fig. 7The influence of temperature (A) and pH (B) on the catalytic activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs. The maximal point in each curve was set as100%.

Fig. 8Kinetic analysis of the as-prepared Fe3O4-MMT NCs using the Michaelis-Menten model (A and B) and double reciprocal plots (C and D).

was0.025mM, 17times lower than that of HRP (0.434mM) 15and much lower than that of other peroxidase mimics, as listed in Table1; this suggested that the Fe3O4-MMT NCs had a quite higher affinity towards TMB. 63


# 3.5Mechanism of the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs


According to the previous studies, the catalytic mechanisms are mostly classified into two kinds: (1) a catalytic process involving

/C15OHradicals45,48,68and (2) a catalytic process involving electron transfer between the substrate and H2O2. 33,65,69-72As abovementioned, it is anticipated that the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs may originate from the generation of /C15OH radicals. 73To confirm this speculation, terephthalic acid (TA) was selected as a fluorescent probe and added to the reaction system H2O2 + Fe3O4-MMT NCs. The TA molecules can easily react with /C15OH to form a strong fluorescent product, 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH), which emits a unique fluorescence at

Then, the incubated solutions were used to test the fluorescence intensity, as shown in Fig. 9. In Fig. 9, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the experimental systems gradually decreases upon increasing the addition amount of Fe3O4-MMT NCs due to the competitive relationship between the Fe3O4-MMT NCs and terephthalic acid in capturing the /C15OH radicals. The competitive ability of terephthalic acid in capturing the /C15OH radicals decreased upon increasing the concentration of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs; this resulted in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity. 47


# 3.6Detection of H2O2using the Fe3O4-MMT NCs


Because the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs is H2O2-concentration dependent, the system can be applied to quantitatively detect H2O2using a simple colorimetric method. A typical dose-response curve at632nm depending on the concentration of H2O2from1m M to1000m M under the optimal conditions is shown in Fig. 10A. In Fig. 10A, it can be obviously found that absorbance at632nm gradually increases upon increasing the H2O2concentration, and the corresponding linear calibration plot is shown in Fig. 10B. The results revealed a good linear relationship between the absorbance of the oxidized TMB and the concentration of H2O2in the range of50-200m M. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be5.1m M(LOD = 3s / k , where s and k are the relative standard deviation of eight parallel controlled measurements and the slope of the linear calibration plots, respectively. In this formula, s = 0.000916and

Furthermore, the visualized variation can be observed by the naked eye, as shown in the inset images corresponding to the different systems containing H2O2at different concentrations, as shown in the inset in Fig. 10B. In addition, we have summarized the detection of H2O2using Fe3O4and other similar peroxidase mimics studied in the literature in Table2. From the reported data and our study, it was found that our Fe3O4-MMT NCs had a lower detection limit and a wider range of H2O2in comparison with the other peroxidase mimics based on Fe3O4nanoparticles using a similar colorimetric method. 51,76Furthermore, it can be seen that this colorimetric sensor has a lower detection limit than some electrochemical and fluorescent sensors previously reported in the literature (Table3).


# 3.7Application in real sample analysis


Universally, it is acknowledged that H2O2can be used as an antiseptic to protect contact lens solutions. However, the amount of residual H2O2in the contact lens solution should be strictly controlled because an excess of H2O2can damage our eyes. Hence, it is important to detect the residual H2O2in real contact lens solutions. Based on the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs and the colorimetric method, the residual H2O2in real contact lens solutions can be detected easily under optimal conditions using the standard addition method. These tests were performed for three times under the same conditions, and the results are listed in Table4. The recovery of H2O2in four samples was in the range from97.6% to103.9%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) values were as low as2.05%, indicating that our proposed colorimetric method demonstrated its feasibility and potential application in the analysis of real samples.


# 4. Conclusions


In summary, Fe3O4-MMT NCs were successfully synthesized via a facile method and exhibited a higher peroxidase-like activity than the Fe3O4NPs or MMT alone. The peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs originates from the generation of /C15OH radicals produced by the decomposition of H2O2. The peroxidase-like activity of this artificial enzyme was dependent on the pH and temperature. The Fe3O4-MMT NCs also exhibit a good affinity towards TMB and H2O2. Additionally, compared to natural enzymes and other nanomaterials, the Fe3O4-MMT NCs, as a novel peroxidase mimetic, showed several advantages, including ease of preparation, low cost, sensitivity, and magnetic properties, which will facilitate the utilization of the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4-MMT NCs in medical diagnostics, biochemistry, and environmental chemistry in the near future.
